titleStmt.title: Letter from Anne MacDonnell to Lady Clonbrock, 21 October 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Anne MacDonnell

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: This is a letter from Anne MacDonnell, honorary secretary of the Irish Women's Association to Augusta Caroline Dillon (née Crofton), Lady Clonbrock. The association was set up to provide assistance to Irish regiments fighting in the Great War and Irish prisoners of war. MacDonnell writes about correspondence with Lieutenant Gilliard of the Connaught Rangers and a prisoner of war in Bulgaria. She also writes that an embargo on sending outer clothes and boots to prisoners and Bulgaria has been lifted but that she does not believe they will be allowed to send blankets.

msIdentifier.collection: Clonbrock Estate Papers, Collection List No 54; Ms. 35,796

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
KENSINGTON PALACE,
LONDON, W. October 21st 1916. Dear Lady Clonbrock, Lieut., Gilliand has written
to me from Bulgaria and I have written to tell
him that it only doubles writing if he writes to
several people about the same object. We have
sent a great deal of underclothing to the
Connaught Ranger Prisoners of War in Bulgaria,
but we were absolutely forbidden to send outer
clothes or boots, or greatcoats. This embargo
was removed on Thursday on which day we immediately
indented for every man. As soon as the Royal
Army Clothing Department give us the clothes,
which will I think be this week, they will be
despatched to the men at once. (of course the men must not
be told for good!give this clothing or it will not be enough I do not for one moment believe that <lb/ that blankets will be allowed to be despatched to
Bulgaria, as the British Censor will not allow
them to be sent to Germany. We shall of course do the clothing for the
Rangers in Bulgaria, just the same as we have sent
to Germany and Turkey.
(2) I have many letters from Lieut., Gilliland, who is
really a most remarkable man and has done extraordinarily
good work for our men in Bulgaria, though I am sure afraid the
men are not as grateful to him as they ought to be. Yours sincerely, Anne McDonnell
Hon. Secretary.
